name: Jimmy Raney
abstract: James Elbert Raney (August 20, 1927 – May 10, 1995) was an American jazz guitarist, born in Louisville, Kentucky, United States, known for his work from 1951 to 1952 and then from 1953 to 1954 with the Red Norvo trio (replacing Tal Farlow) and, during the same time period, with Stan Getz. In 1954 and 1955, he won the DownBeat Critics' Po…

about_quotes.speaker: Mandel Johnny
about_quotes.evidence: But Artie’s band, we had Jimmy Raney in that band so it was, yeah, it was just an all- around good band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mandel Johnny — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jim Hall
about_quotes.evidence: we had a trio... with Jimmy, we had this trio that was really pretty inventive
about_quotes.doc_title: Jim Hall — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Crow
about_quotes.evidence: And then Jimmy Raney, I came to work with him with Stan Getz wasn’t working, he had been Stan’s regular guitar player, and he took me on to Stan’s band.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bill Crow interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, October 18, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Gary Burton
about_quotes.evidence: So here I am on the stage with this quartet... So we start playing, and Jimmy Raney comps constantly throughout everything.
about_quotes.doc_title: Gary Burton — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Sam Most
about_quotes.evidence: I had a string quartet album at that time I guess in the 50s, and I had Jimmy Raney and Art Farmer’s brother...
about_quotes.doc_title: Sam Most interviewed by Monk Rowe, Los Angeles, California, February 15, 1999
